### Objective 1: Develop and optimize conditions for antibody-drug conjugate click reactions as model systems

Objective 1 aims to establish and optimize reaction conditions for click chemistry within biomolecular condensates, focusing on environmental sustainability and reaction efficiency. The initial phase will involve the formation of LLPS using established methods, varying parameters such as polymer concentration (0.1-10 mg/mL), salt concentration (0-500 mM NaCl), and temperature (4-37°C) to identify conditions conducive to condensate formation. Orthogonal biopolymers will be tested to optimize LLPS formation. The formation of condensates will be confirmed using microscopy techniques, including phase-contrast and fluorescence microscopy, to visualize droplet morphology and measure size distribution. Critical parameters for click chemistry, including catalyst concentration (CuSO4: 0.1-1 mol%), ligand concentration (TBTA: 0.1-1 mol%), and reactant stoichiometry (alkyne:azide ratios from 1:1 to 1:10), will be systematically optimized. Reactions will be performed in aqueous buffers (pH 6-8) to maintain biocompatibility and minimize environmental impact. Reaction progress will be monitored using HPLC and mass spectrometry to quantify product formation and identify any side products. The efficiency of the click reactions within condensates will be compared to reactions performed in traditional organic solvents (e.g., DMF, DMSO) to evaluate the green chemistry benefits. Metrics for evaluation will include reaction yield, reaction rate, and E-factor (environmental factor), which measures the amount of waste generated per unit of product. Compatibility between click reaction conditions and LLPS formation will be assessed by monitoring condensate stability and morphology during the reaction. Conditions that maintain stable condensates and high reaction efficiency will be prioritized. The optimized conditions from this objective will be used in subsequent objectives to study encapsulation and kinetics.

#### 1.1: Develop and optimize reaction conditions of two click reaction models involving formation of antibody-drug conjugates

Reaction optimization will employ a design of experiments (DoE) approach, specifically a full factorial design, to systematically evaluate the impact of multiple parameters on reaction yield and selectivity. Parameters to be optimized include catalyst loading (0.05-5 mol%), ligand concentration (0.05-5 mol%), temperature (20-60 °C), and reaction time (1-24 hours). The reaction will be monitored using quantitative HPLC-MS utilizing a C18 column with a gradient mobile phase of acetonitrile and water containing 0.1% formic acid; product detection will be achieved via mass spectrometry in selected ion monitoring (SIM) mode. Green chemistry metrics, such as atom economy and E-factor, will be calculated to assess the environmental impact of each condition; atom economy will be determined by dividing the molecular weight of the desired product by the sum of the molecular weights of all reactants, while the E-factor will be calculated as the ratio of the mass of waste to the mass of product. The optimal conditions will be defined as those that provide the highest yield of the desired ADC product with minimal waste generation, while maintaining LLPS stability as assessed by microscopy. Statistical analysis (ANOVA) will be used to determine the significance of each parameter on the reaction outcome, with p-values < 0.05 considered statistically significant. The optimized conditions will be validated through triplicate experiments to ensure reproducibility. **[MISSING INFORMATION: Details about specific HPLC-MS methods]**

#### 1.2: Develop and optimize conditions for LLPS and condensate formation under reaction conditions

LLPS optimization will focus on tuning peptide sequence characteristics to enhance condensate formation under reaction conditions. Peptide design will incorporate charged residues (lysine, glutamic acid) and aromatic residues (phenylalanine, tyrosine) to modulate electrostatic and π-π interactions, respectively. Specific peptide sequences to be tested include Ac-FKFEFKFE-NH2, Ac-FEFKFEFK-NH2, and Ac-FXYEFXYEFXYE-NH2, where X represents either tyrosine or phenylalanine. The impact of these modifications on LLPS will be assessed by monitoring droplet size, morphology, and stability using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP). DLS will measure hydrodynamic radius and polydispersity index, while FRAP will quantify the diffusion coefficients of the peptides within the condensates. Condensate stability will be evaluated under varying salt concentrations (0-500 mM NaCl) and temperatures (4-37°C) to mimic reaction conditions. The internal environment of condensates will be characterized using fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS) to determine the diffusion coefficients of encapsulated reactants. Partitioning coefficients of reactants and catalysts into the condensates will be quantified using confocal microscopy (Leica SP8) and fluorescence intensity measurements, with data analysis performed using ImageJ. These parameters will be correlated with reaction kinetics to identify optimal conditions for enhanced reaction rates. The relationship between condensate properties and reaction efficiency will be investigated by varying the concentrations of peptides to control the density and viscosity of the condensates. Specifically, we will modify peptide sequences to enhance or diminish pi-pi interactions and electrostatic interactions, observing changes in LLPS behavior and reaction rates.

### Objective 2: Characterization of reactant and product encapsulation

Objective 2 focuses on characterizing molecular partitioning within peptide-based condensates to optimize encapsulation efficiency and product separation, aligning with green chemistry principles by minimizing solvent use and waste. The partitioning behavior of reactants, catalysts, and products will be quantitatively assessed using confocal microscopy and fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS). Specifically, we will measure the concentrations of each species inside and outside the condensates to determine partition coefficients (Kpart = [inside]/[outside]). Peptide sequence modifications, including variations in charged and aromatic residues, will be systematically evaluated for their impact on selective molecular recruitment. For example, incorporating multiple arginine residues may enhance the encapsulation of negatively charged catalysts. The effect of condensate material properties, such as viscosity and surface tension, on molecular partitioning dynamics will be investigated using fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP) and microfluidic techniques. FRAP will quantify the diffusion rates of encapsulated molecules, while microfluidics will allow for precise control over condensate size and composition. Encapsulation efficiency will be quantified as the percentage of a target molecule encapsulated within the condensates, measured using confocal microscopy and image analysis software (e.g., ImageJ). Product separation will be assessed by measuring the ratio of product to reactants inside and outside the condensates using HPLC-MS. Optimization strategies will focus on tuning peptide sequence and condensate composition to maximize encapsulation efficiency and product separation. Molecular dynamics simulations will complement experimental studies, providing insights into the interactions between peptides and encapsulated molecules. These simulations will be performed using GROMACS, employing the CHARMM36 force field, with a simulation time of 100 ns per system. The simulation data will be analyzed to determine binding affinities and interaction energies between the peptides and target molecules. The results from this objective will inform the design of more efficient and sustainable catalytic systems in subsequent objectives. The encapsulation characteristics determined here are crucial for understanding reaction behavior in Objective 3, and encapsulation efficiency data informs system sustainability assessment in Objective 4. Specifically, we will test arginine concentrations ranging from 5-20 mol% relative to the total peptide concentration to optimize the encapsulation of negatively charged catalysts. Similarly, the ratio of phenylalanine to tyrosine residues will be varied from 1:0 to 0:1 to fine-tune π-π interactions and modulate partitioning. HPLC-MS analysis will be conducted using a reversed-phase C18 column (e.g., Agilent Zorbax Eclipse Plus C18) with a gradient elution of acetonitrile and water, both containing 0.1% formic acid, to quantify the concentrations of reactants and products. The flow rate will be maintained at 0.3 mL/min, and detection will be performed using electrospray ionization (ESI) in positive or negative mode, depending on the analyte. The column temperature will be maintained at 40 °C to ensure consistent separation. These parameters will be optimized to achieve baseline separation and accurate quantification of all relevant species. The impact of condensate viscosity on molecular partitioning will be further investigated by varying the concentration of crowding agents, such as PEG (2-10% w/v), and measuring changes in diffusion coefficients using FCS. Surface tension will be modulated by incorporating surfactants (e.g., Tween 20) at concentrations ranging from 0.01-0.1% w/v, and its effect on condensate morphology and molecular partitioning will be assessed using microscopy. These comprehensive analyses will provide a detailed understanding of the factors governing molecular partitioning in peptide-based condensates, enabling the rational design of optimized catalytic systems.

#### 2.1: Analysing the reactant and product encapsulation efficiency (EE) in condensates

Encapsulation efficiency (EE) will be determined using UV-Vis spectroscopy (Agilent Cary 60) at 260 nm and 280 nm using a quartz cuvette to quantify the concentration of encapsulated molecules before and after condensate removal via centrifugation (14,000 x g for 10 min). EE will be calculated as the ratio of encapsulated molecule concentration to the initial concentration. Fluorescence spectroscopy (Horiba Fluoromax-4) with excitation/emission wavelengths optimized for each fluorophore (e.g., Alexa Fluor 488 conjugated to peptides) will be employed to measure the fluorescence intensity of labeled molecules inside and outside the condensates, providing an alternative measure of EE. Partition coefficients (Kpart) will be calculated from these measurements using OriginPro software. Measurement validation will involve comparing EE values obtained from UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, as well as orthogonal methods such as dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS with a 633 nm laser to assess hydrodynamic size changes upon encapsulation. Temporal stability of encapsulation will be assessed by measuring EE at various time points (0, 1, 3, 6, 12, and 24 hours) to determine the rate of molecule leakage from the condensates. Statistical analysis (t-tests, ANOVA with Tukey post-hoc tests) will be used to compare EE values under different conditions, with p < 0.05 indicating statistical significance.

#### 2.2: Optimization of reactant EE in the condensate by tuning the peptide composition and reaction conditions

Reactant encapsulation will be optimized through systematic variation of peptide sequence and environmental conditions. Peptide design will focus on incorporating unnatural amino acids with tailored hydrophobic or electrostatic properties to enhance binding affinity for specific reactants. Specifically, norleucine will be used to increase hydrophobicity, while diaminobutyric acid will be incorporated to enhance electrostatic interactions. A library of peptides with systematically varied sequences will be synthesized using solid-phase peptide synthesis (SPPS). Encapsulation efficiency (EE) will be quantified using confocal microscopy and HPLC-MS, correlating peptide sequence with reactant partitioning coefficients. Environmental parameters, including salt concentration (0-500 mM), pH (6-8), and temperature (4-37°C), will be optimized to maximize EE and condensate stability. Performance metrics will include EE, condensate size, and reactant diffusion coefficients within condensates, measured using FCS. Structure-function relationships will be elucidated by correlating peptide sequence, condensate properties, and EE, guiding further sequence optimization using algorithms such as Bayesian optimization with a Gaussian process surrogate model to efficiently navigate the sequence space and identify optimal candidates. Trade-offs between EE, condensate size, and reactant diffusion will be evaluated using Pareto analysis to identify optimal conditions that balance these competing objectives.

#### 2.3: Optimization of product EE in the condensate by tuning the peptide composition and reaction conditions aiming at product exclusion following separation of the phases

Product exclusion optimization will exploit differences in molecular size, charge, and hydrophobicity between the desired product (ADC) and reactants. Selective partitioning will be achieved by tuning the peptide sequence to create condensates with specific affinity for the reactants, while excluding the larger ADC. Phase separation dynamics will be controlled by adjusting salt concentration (50-500 mM NaCl) and temperature (4-37°C) to promote efficient product release. Product recovery will involve gentle washing steps using biocompatible buffers such as Tris or PBS to extract the ADC from the condensates, followed by HPLC purification using a C18 column with a gradient mobile phase of acetonitrile and water. Separation efficiency will be quantified by measuring the ratio of ADC to reactants in the wash fractions using HPLC-MS. System stability during extraction will be maintained by optimizing buffer composition and temperature to prevent condensate dissolution or aggregation. The impact of product exclusion on microscopy analysis is detailed in Task 3.2, and the effect of separation efficiency on sustainability is analyzed in Task 4.1.

### Objective 3: Characterization of reactions in condensates

### Objective 3: Reaction kinetics and mechanism in condensates

Objective 3 aims to elucidate the reaction kinetics and mechanisms of click chemistry reactions within biomolecular condensates, providing insights into how phase separation influences reaction dynamics. The kinetics of click reactions will be quantitatively assessed using HPLC-MS (Agilent 1260 Infinity II) with a reversed-phase C18 column (e.g., Waters XBridge C18, 3.5 μm, 4.6 x 150 mm) to monitor reactant consumption and product formation over time. The mobile phase will consist of acetonitrile and water, both containing 0.1% formic acid, with a gradient elution. Detection will be performed using electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) in selected ion monitoring (SIM) mode. Reactions will be performed under varying conditions of condensate composition, temperature, and reactant concentrations to determine the rate constants and activation energies. The influence of condensate viscosity on reaction rates will be investigated using fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS) to measure the diffusion coefficients of reactants within the condensates. The reaction mechanism will be probed using kinetic isotope effect (KIE) studies, substituting hydrogen with deuterium at the alkyne C-H position and the azide α-position to identify rate-limiting steps. KIE values will be calculated by comparing the reaction rates of protiated and deuterated substrates (kH/kD). Computational modeling, including molecular dynamics simulations and transition state calculations, will complement experimental studies, providing insights into the reaction pathway and the role of the condensate environment. Specifically, we will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations using Gaussian 16 with the B3LYP functional and 6-31G(d) basis set to model the transition states of the click reactions in both the presence and absence of the condensate environment. Molecular dynamics simulations will be performed using GROMACS 2020 with the CHARMM36 force field, with a simulation time of 500 ns per system. The system will be solvated with TIP3P water molecules, and counterions will be added to neutralize the system. The temperature will be maintained at 300 K using the V-rescale thermostat, and the pressure will be maintained at 1 bar using the Parrinello-Rahman barostat. The activation energies obtained from these calculations will be compared with experimental values to validate the computational model. The impact of condensate properties on reaction kinetics will be further investigated by varying the concentrations of peptides (e.g., Ac-FKFEFKFE-NH2 and Ac-FEFKFEFK-NH2) to control the density and viscosity of the condensates. The optimized conditions from Objectives 1 and 2 will be used as a starting point and further refined based on the kinetic data obtained in this objective. The kinetic parameters determined here are essential for assessing system scalability in Objective 4, and the mechanistic insights support commercialization strategy in Objective 5. The effect of reactant partitioning on reaction rates will be deconvoluted by correlating partitioning coefficients (from Objective 2) with reaction rates. The role of water activity within condensates will be investigated by adding osmolytes (e.g., glycerol) and measuring changes in reaction rates. The effect of crowding on reaction kinetics will be assessed by comparing reaction rates in condensates with those in bulk solution at equivalent reactant concentrations. The results from this objective will provide a comprehensive understanding of the factors governing reaction kinetics and mechanisms in biomolecular condensates, enabling the rational design of optimized catalytic systems. The temperature dependence of reaction rates will be analyzed using Arrhenius plots to determine activation energies and pre-exponential factors. The effect of catalyst concentration on reaction rates will be investigated to identify potential catalyst inhibition or saturation effects. The ionic strength dependence of reaction rates will be assessed by varying the concentration of inert salts (e.g., NaCl) and measuring changes in reaction rates. These comprehensive analyses will provide a detailed understanding of the factors governing reaction kinetics in peptide-based condensates, enabling the rational design of optimized catalytic systems.

#### 3.1: Quantitative analysis of reaction kinetics, conversion and initial rate in condensates

Reaction kinetics will be quantitatively analyzed using HPLC-MS (Agilent 1260 Infinity II) to monitor the change in reactant and product concentrations over time, employing a C18 column (e.g., Waters XBridge C18, 3.5 μm, 4.6 x 150 mm) with a gradient mobile phase of acetonitrile and water containing 0.1% formic acid. The gradient elution program will start at 5% acetonitrile, increase to 95% over 20 minutes, hold at 95% for 5 minutes, and then return to 5% in 2 minutes, with a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min. Detection will be performed using an Agilent 6120 quadrupole MS in positive or negative electrospray ionization (ESI) mode, with a capillary voltage of 3.5 kV, a fragmentor voltage of 70 V, and a scan range of 100-1000 m/z. Initial velocity will be determined from the slope of the product formation curve at early time points (0-10 min) to minimize the impact of product inhibition or side reactions. Conversion rate will be calculated as the percentage of reactant converted to product at a given time point. Data will be normalized to account for variations in condensate volume and reactant partitioning using internal standards (e.g., deuterated substrate analogs). Control experiments will be performed in the absence of condensates to determine the effect of phase separation on reaction kinetics. The Arrhenius equation will be used to model the temperature dependence of reaction rates (25-50°C), providing insights into the activation energy of the reaction within condensates. Data will be fitted to appropriate kinetic models (e.g., Michaelis-Menten, Langmuir-Hinshelwood) using GraphPad Prism 9.0 to determine kinetic parameters (kcat, Km, catalytic efficiency). Linear or non-linear regression analysis will be employed to determine the best fit, and catalytic efficiency (kcat/Km) will be calculated. Internal standards and calibration curves using known concentrations of reactants and products will validate kinetic measurements. Specifically, a series of standard solutions will be prepared, and their concentrations will be plotted against the corresponding peak areas from HPLC-MS analysis. Kinetics data guides optimization strategies in Task 3.3.

#### 3.2: Microscopy analysis of reaction kinetics to elucidate product localization in the two phases

To visualize product localization and track temporal dynamics, confocal microscopy (Zeiss LSM 880) with Airyscan detection will be employed, providing enhanced spatial resolution (140 nm laterally and 400 nm axially). Time-lapse imaging will capture reaction progression within condensates, with image acquisition every 1-5 minutes for up to 24 hours, using 405 nm, 488 nm, and 561 nm laser lines for multi-channel fluorescence imaging. Fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP) will quantify molecular mobility and exchange rates, with a 488 nm laser for bleaching and recovery monitored over 30-60 seconds. Image analysis will be performed using FIJI/ImageJ, with custom macros developed to quantify phase-specific product distribution and condensate morphology, including measurements of circularity, aspect ratio, and area. Co-localization analysis, using Pearson's correlation coefficient, will assess the spatial correlation between reactants and products. Microscopy data will be integrated with kinetic measurements from HPLC-MS to correlate reaction rates with spatial distribution. Localization data influences optimization approaches in Task 3.3, and phase distribution information impacts sustainability assessment in Task 4.1.

#### 3.3: Optimization of condensate reactors composition and reaction conditions to increase reaction efficiency

Reaction condition optimization will focus on varying temperature (20-60°C), catalyst concentration (0.01-1 mol% CuSO4 with TBTA ligand), and reactant stoichiometry (1:1 to 1:10 alkyne:azide) to maximize reaction rates and yields. Peptide sequence will be modified to incorporate unnatural amino acids with tailored hydrophobic (norleucine) or electrostatic (diaminobutyric acid) properties to enhance binding affinity for specific reactants. The reactions will be monitored using quantitative HPLC-MS, utilizing a C18 column with a gradient mobile phase of acetonitrile and water containing 0.1% formic acid; product detection will be achieved via mass spectrometry in selected ion monitoring (SIM) mode. Kinetic parameters (rate constants, activation energies) will be determined by fitting the data to Michaelis-Menten kinetics models. Statistical analysis (ANOVA) will be used to assess the significance of each parameter on the reaction rate, with p < 0.05 considered statistically significant. Condensate properties, including viscosity (measured by DLS) and surface tension, will be modulated by incorporating surfactants (e.g., Tween 20) at concentrations ranging from 0.01-0.1% w/v, and its effect on reaction efficiency will be assessed using microscopy. Green chemistry metrics, such as atom economy and E-factor, will be calculated to assess the environmental impact of each condition. The optimal conditions will be validated through triplicate experiments to ensure reproducibility. **[MISSING INFORMATION: Details about specific HPLC-MS methods]**

### Objective 4: System sustainability and scalability

### Objective 4: Sustainability and Scalability Analysis

Objective 4 will focus on a comprehensive sustainability and scalability assessment of the optimized click chemistry reactions within biomolecular condensates. Sustainability metrics, including atom economy, E-factor, and reaction mass efficiency (RME), will be quantitatively evaluated across multiple reaction cycles (up to 10 cycles) to determine the long-term environmental impact. The E-factor will be calculated as the ratio of the mass of waste to the mass of product, while RME will be determined as the percentage of reactant mass that ends up in the final product. Green chemistry principles will guide the selection of solvents and reagents, prioritizing water as the primary reaction medium and minimizing the use of hazardous substances such as DMF and DMSO. Condensate stability and function will be monitored during scale-up operations (1 mL to 100 mL) to ensure consistent performance. Scale-up parameters, such as mixing speed (50-500 rpm in a stirred-tank reactor), temperature control (±0.5°C), and reactor design (specifically, a jacketed glass reactor for precise temperature control), will be optimized to maintain condensate integrity and reaction efficiency. Product separation efficiency will be assessed at different scales using HPLC-MS (Agilent 1260 Infinity II with a C18 column, gradient elution of acetonitrile/water with 0.1% formic acid, flow rate of 0.3 mL/min, and ESI-MS detection) to quantify product purity and identify any degradation products. Reaction kinetics and yields will be compared between laboratory and scaled-up conditions to evaluate the impact of scale on reaction performance. Critical dependencies with reaction optimization (Objective 1) and commercialization objectives (Objective 5) will be addressed by integrating sustainability and scalability considerations into the overall process design. Scale-up findings may require refinement of reaction conditions in Objective 3. The sustainability data directly informs commercialization strategy in Objective 5. The effect of separation efficiency on sustainability is analyzed in Task 4.1. The localization data from Task 3.2 impacts sustainability assessment in Task 4.1. The optimized conditions from Objective 3 will be used as a starting point and further refined based on the sustainability and scalability data obtained in this objective. The long-term stability of the condensates will be evaluated by monitoring their size, morphology, and activity over a period of several weeks. The reusability of the condensates will be assessed by recycling them through multiple reaction cycles and measuring any loss in activity. The energy consumption of the reaction will be minimized by optimizing reaction conditions and using energy-efficient equipment. The waste generated by the reaction will be minimized by using catalytic amounts of reagents and by recovering and reusing solvents and catalysts. The overall goal of this objective is to develop a sustainable and scalable click chemistry process that can be used for the synthesis of antibody-drug conjugates and other valuable products.

#### 4.1: Analysing reaction sustainability and ability to perform multiple reaction cycles following product separation

System sustainability will be assessed by monitoring condensate stability (size, morphology via microscopy), product separation efficiency (HPLC), and yield consistency (HPLC) across ten reaction cycles. Condensate stability will be quantified by measuring the change in droplet size and morphology using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and microscopy. Product separation efficiency will be determined by measuring the ratio of product to reactants in the separated phases using HPLC-MS. Yield consistency will be evaluated by calculating the standard deviation of product yields across multiple cycles. Acceptance criteria include <10% change in condensate size, >95% product separation efficiency, and <5% standard deviation in yield. Potential failure modes include condensate degradation, catalyst leaching, and product contamination. Mitigation strategies involve optimizing buffer composition, catalyst loading, and separation techniques. Specifically, catalyst leaching will be addressed by employing a solid-supported catalyst or by modifying the peptide sequence to enhance catalyst binding within the condensate. System regeneration between cycles will involve washing the condensates with a buffer solution (e.g., 50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.4) to remove unreacted substrates and products, followed by re-equilibration in fresh reaction buffer. HPLC-MS analysis will be conducted using a reversed-phase C18 column (e.g., Agilent Zorbax Eclipse Plus C18) with a gradient elution of acetonitrile and water, both containing 0.1% formic acid, to quantify the concentrations of reactants and products. The flow rate will be maintained at 0.3 mL/min, and detection will be performed using electrospray ionization (ESI) in positive or negative mode, depending on the analyte. Cycling capability impacts market potential assessment in Task 5.1, and sustainability metrics inform scale-up parameters in Task 4.2.

#### 4.2: Analysing condensate formation and reaction performance at larger scales at the mL-L range

Scale-up parameters, including mixing dynamics, heat transfer, and mass transport, will be analyzed for their effects on condensate formation and stability. Mixing speed will be varied from 50 to 500 rpm in a stirred-tank reactor, informed by computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations predicting optimal shear rates for condensate dispersion, with condensate size and distribution measured using dynamic light scattering (DLS). Heat transfer efficiency will be evaluated by monitoring temperature gradients within the reactor using thermocouples and a jacketed reactor with a recirculating chiller to maintain temperature uniformity (±0.5°C). Mass transport limitations will be assessed by measuring the diffusion coefficients of reactants and products within the condensates using fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS). Condensate formation and stability will be evaluated at different scales (1 mL to 100 mL) using microscopy techniques, including phase-contrast and fluorescence microscopy, to visualize droplet morphology and measure size distribution. Operational parameters, such as temperature, pH, and salt concentration, will be optimized to maintain reaction efficiency during scale-up. Critical engineering challenges, such as maintaining uniform mixing and temperature control, will be addressed through reactor design and process optimization, including the implementation of baffles to enhance mixing. The relationship between operational parameters and reaction efficiency will be determined by correlating reaction rates and yields with mixing speed, temperature, and mass transport coefficients. Condensate stability will be assessed by monitoring changes in size, morphology, and activity over time. The effect of condensate properties on reaction kinetics will be evaluated by correlating condensate viscosity and surface tension with reaction rates. Data from Task 5.2 will be used for competitive analysis, and performance data will inform market strategy in Task 5.3.

The successful execution of this project hinges on the strategic allocation and effective management of resources. This section provides a detailed breakdown of the budget, personnel, equipment, and infrastructure required to achieve the project's objectives, ensuring cost-effectiveness and maximizing the impact of the proposed research.

**Budget Justification**

The total budget requested for this project is $750,000. This budget encompasses personnel costs, equipment costs, consumables, travel, training, publication costs, and indirect costs. Each category is carefully justified below, demonstrating a clear alignment with the project's objectives and a commitment to fiscal responsibility.

* **Personnel:** A dedicated team of researchers, including 2 Postdoctoral Researchers, 3 Graduate Students, and 2 Research Assistants, will be essential for the successful execution of the project. Their expertise in chemistry, materials science, and engineering will drive the research forward. The total personnel costs are estimated at $450,000 over the project duration. This includes salaries, fringe benefits, and associated employment costs. The allocation of personnel time is directly tied to specific tasks within each objective, ensuring efficient use of human resources. For example, Postdoctoral Researchers will lead the experimental design and data analysis efforts in Objectives 1-3, while Graduate Students will focus on the synthesis and characterization of materials in Objectives 2 and 4. Research Assistants will provide crucial support in sample preparation, instrument maintenance, and data collection across all objectives. Specifically, one Postdoctoral Researcher will focus on tasks 1.1, 2.1, and 3.1, involving reaction optimization and quantitative analysis using HPLC-MS. The other Postdoctoral Researcher will focus on microscopy analysis (Task 3.2) and scale-up studies (Task 4.2). Two Graduate Students will be dedicated to peptide synthesis and condensate formation (Tasks 1.2 and 2.2), while the third will focus on product separation and sustainability analysis (Tasks 2.3 and 4.1). The Research Assistants will support all objectives by assisting with sample preparation, data collection, and instrument maintenance.
* **Equipment:** Access to state-of-the-art equipment is critical for conducting high-resolution experiments and obtaining reliable data. We request $150,000 for the purchase and maintenance of essential equipment, including HPLC-MS systems (Agilent 1260 Infinity II, $75,000), confocal microscopes (Zeiss LSM 880, $50,000), dynamic light scattering instruments (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS, $25,000), and other analytical instruments. A detailed list of equipment and their respective costs is provided in Appendix A. The requested equipment will be used extensively throughout the project to characterize the structure, properties, and performance of the developed materials and devices. For example, the HPLC-MS systems will be used to analyze the composition and purity of the synthesized materials (Objectives 1, 2, 3, and 4), while the confocal microscopes will be used to visualize the morphology and structure of the materials at the nanoscale (Objectives 2 and 3). The dynamic light scattering instruments will be used to measure the size and stability of the materials in solution (Objectives 1, 2, and 4). Specifically, the Agilent 1260 Infinity II HPLC-MS system will be equipped with a Waters XBridge C18 column (3.5 μm, 4.6 x 150 mm) for quantitative analysis of reaction kinetics and product separation. The Zeiss LSM 880 confocal microscope will be used with Airyscan detection for enhanced spatial resolution in Task 3.2. The equipment will be housed in the Materials Science and Engineering Laboratory and the Chemistry Department Analytical Facility and will be available to all project personnel.
* **Consumables:** The project requires a range of chemical reagents, materials, and supplies for synthesis, characterization, and device fabrication. The estimated cost for consumables is $50,000 over the project duration. This includes perovskite precursors, organic ligands, solvents, substrates, and other essential materials. A detailed list of consumables and their respective costs is provided in Appendix B. We will prioritize the use of environmentally friendly and sustainable materials whenever possible to minimize the environmental impact of the project. Specific consumables include acetonitrile, water, formic acid (HPLC grade), copper sulfate (CuSO4), TBTA ligand, norleucine, and diaminobutyric acid. These will be used in Objectives 1, 2, and 3 for reaction optimization, encapsulation studies, and kinetic analyses. We will also require C18 columns (e.g., Agilent Zorbax Eclipse Plus C18) for HPLC-MS analysis in Tasks 2.1, 3.1, and 4.1.
* **Travel:** Travel funds are requested to facilitate collaboration with external research groups, attend scientific conferences, and disseminate the project's findings. The estimated cost for travel is $25,000 over the project duration. This includes travel to national and international conferences, as well as travel to visit collaborators at other universities and research institutions. We will prioritize travel to conferences and workshops that are relevant to the project's objectives and that provide opportunities to present our research findings to a wider audience. Specifically, we plan to attend the ACS National Meeting and the Materials Research Society (MRS) Fall Meeting to present our findings on condensate-based catalysis and sustainable chemistry.
* **Other Expenses:** This category includes publication costs, software licenses, and other miscellaneous expenses. The estimated cost for other expenses is $25,000 over the project duration. This includes publication fees for peer-reviewed journals, software licenses for data analysis and modeling (e.g., OriginPro, GraphPad Prism), and other miscellaneous expenses such as equipment maintenance and repair. Specifically, we will allocate funds for open-access publication fees to ensure broad dissemination of our research findings. We will also purchase licenses for GROMACS 2020 and Gaussian 16 for molecular dynamics simulations and DFT calculations in Objective 3.**Infrastructure and Facilities**

This project will leverage existing infrastructure and facilities at the University of California, Berkeley, including well-equipped laboratories, cleanrooms, and shared instrument facilities. These resources provide a strong foundation for conducting cutting-edge research and ensuring the project's success. Specifically, we will utilize the Materials Science and Engineering Laboratory for materials synthesis and characterization, the Chemistry Department Analytical Facility for device fabrication, and the Advanced Bioimaging Center for advanced microscopy and spectroscopy measurements. These facilities are staffed by experienced technicians and researchers who will provide training and support to project personnel.

**Cost-Effectiveness Analysis**

The proposed budget represents a cost-effective approach to achieving the project's objectives. We have carefully considered the costs of all resources and have prioritized the most essential items. We have also sought to leverage existing resources and infrastructure whenever possible to minimize costs. The project's potential impact on the field of sustainable chemistry and its contribution to the development of more efficient and environmentally friendly chemical processes justify the requested resources. The detailed budget justification provided above demonstrates a clear understanding of the project's resource needs and a commitment to fiscal responsibility. The strategic allocation of personnel, equipment, and infrastructure will ensure the project's success and maximize its impact on the field.
